blocks:
Ayahs

لَآ أُقْسِمُ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ (١)
I swear by this town, (1)
وَأَنتَ حِلٌّۢ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ (٢)
as you reside in this town; (2)
وَوَالِدٍ وَمَا وَلَدَ (٣)
[and] by the father and him whom he begot: (3)
لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ فِى كَبَدٍ (٤)
certainly We created man in travail. (4)
أَيَحْسَبُ أَن لَّن يَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ أَحَدٌ (٥)
Does he suppose that no one will ever have power over him? (5)
يَقُولُ أَهْلَكْتُ مَالًا لُّبَدًا (٦)
He says, ‘I have squandered immense wealth.’ (6)
أَيَحْسَبُ أَن لَّمْ يَرَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ (٧)
Does he suppose that no one sees him? (7)
أَلَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُۥ عَيْنَيْنِ (٨)
Have We not made for him two eyes, (8)
وَلِسَانًا وَشَفَتَيْنِ (٩)
a tongue, and two lips, (9)
وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلنَّجْدَيْنِ (١٠)
and shown him the two paths [of good and evil]? (10)
فَلَا ٱقْتَحَمَ ٱلْعَقَبَةَ (١١)
Yet he has not embarked upon the uphill task. (11)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْعَقَبَةُ (١٢)
And what will show you what is the uphill task? (12)
فَكُّ رَقَبَةٍ (١٣)
[It is] the freeing of a slave, (13)
أَوْ إِطْعَـٰمٌ فِى يَوْمٍ ذِى مَسْغَبَةٍ (١٤)
or feeding, during days of [general] starvation, (14)
يَتِيمًا ذَا مَقْرَبَةٍ (١٥)
an orphan among relatives (15)
أَوْ مِسْكِينًا ذَا مَتْرَبَةٍ (١٦)
or a needy man in desolation, (16)
ثُمَّ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلْمَرْحَمَةِ (١٧)
while being one of those who have faith and enjoin one another to patience, and enjoin one another to compassion. (17)
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَيْمَنَةِ (١٨)
They are the People of the Right Hand. (18)
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا هُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَشْـَٔمَةِ (١٩)
But those who defy Our signs, they are the People of the Left Hand. (19)
عَلَيْهِمْ نَارٌ مُّؤْصَدَةٌۢ (٢٠)
A closed Fire will be [imposed] upon them. (20)

(Commentary)

The Surah mentions that the creation of man is built upon toil and hardship (التعب والمشقة). Thus, one finds no affair among the affairs of life except that it is coupled with the bitterness of labor and exhaustion, from the moment the spirit enters his body until he dies. He has no rest that is devoid of toil and hardship, nor any happiness pure from misery and ill fortune (المشأمة), except in the Abode of the Hereafter with Allah.

Therefore, let him bear the weight of divine obligations through patience (الصبر) in obedience and against disobedience. Let him strive in spreading mercy (الرحمة) upon those afflicted by the calamities of time, such as the orphan, the poor, the sick, and their like, so that he may be among the People of the Right Hand (أصحاب الميمنة). Otherwise, his end shall be like his beginning, and he will be among the People of the Left Hand (أصحاب المشأمة), upon whom is a closed fire.

The context of the verses of this Surah resembles the Meccan context, which supports the view that the Surah is Meccan; indeed, some have claimed a consensus (الإجماع) on this. It has also been said that the Surah is Medinan, but the context does not support this. Another view is that it is Medinan except for the first four verses, as will be discussed in the following traditional inquiry, Allah the Exalted willing.

His saying, the Exalted: لا أُقْسِمُ بِهذَا الْبَلَدِ — I swear by this town (90:1). They have mentioned that what is meant by "this town" is Mecca. This is supported by the Meccan context of the Surah and His saying: وَوالِدٍ وَما وَلَدَ — and by the father and him whom he begot (90:3), specifically based on the view that the "father" refers to Ibrahim (peace be upon him), as will follow.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْتَ حِلٌّ بِهذَا الْبَلَدِ — as you are an inhabitant of this town (90:2). This is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to "this town." The explicit noun is used in place of a pronoun in His saying "by this town" (بِهذَا الْبَلَدِ) to indicate the greatness of its status and the care for its affair, for it is the Sacred City. The word hill (حِلّ) is a verbal noun like hulul (الحلول), meaning residing and settling in a place, and here the verbal noun carries the meaning of the active participle (الفاعل).

The meaning is: I swear by this town while you are a resident (حالّ) and inhabitant within it. In this, there is an intimation of the honor bestowed upon Mecca by his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) presence therein, and its being his birthplace and residence.

It has also been said that the sentence is a parenthetical clause (جملة معترضة) between the oath and the object of the oath. In this view, hill (الحل) refers to one who is "permissible" (المستحل), meaning one whose sanctity is not respected. The author of al-Kashshaf said: "A parenthetical clause was inserted between the oath and the object of the oath with His saying: وَأَنْتَ حِلٌّ بِهذَا الْبَلَدِ; meaning, it is part of the hardship (المكابدة) that someone like you, despite your great sanctity, is treated as 'permissible' (يستحل) in this Sacred City, just as game is treated as permissible outside the Sanctuary. It is narrated from Shurahbil: 'They forbid the killing of game therein and the lopping (١) of its trees, yet they deem your expulsion and killing permissible.' In this is a strengthening for the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and an encouragement to endure what he was suffering from the people of Mecca, and an expression of wonder at their state in their enmity toward him." End quote.

He then said: "Or the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was consoled by the oath by his city—that man is never free from enduring hardships—and the parenthetical clause was a promise of the conquest of Mecca to complete the consolation and relief for him. Thus He said: وَأَنْتَ حِلٌّ بِهذَا الْبَلَدِ, meaning: you shall be 'permissible' (حل) in it in the future, doing therein what you wish of killing and capturing..." until the end of his remarks. The gist of this is the interpretation of hill (الحل) as muhill (المحل), the opposite of muhrim (المحرم). The meaning would be: We shall make it permissible for you for a time on the day of the conquest of Mecca, so you may fight and kill whom you wish therein.

His saying, the Exalted: وَوالِدٍ وَما وَلَدَ — and by the father and him whom he begot (90:3). The necessity of a certain type of correspondence and connection between the oath and the object of the oath requires that the "father and him whom he begot" be someone between whom and the town sworn by there is a manifest relationship. This applies to Ibrahim and his son Isma’il (peace be upon them), for they were the primary causes for the building of the town of Mecca and the builders of the Sacred House. The Exalted said: وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْراهِيمُ الْقَواعِدَ مِنَ الْبَيْتِ وَإِسْماعِيلُ — As Ibrahim raised the foundations of the House with Isma’il (2:127). Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was the one who asked Allah to make Mecca a secure town; the Exalted said: وَإِذْ قالَ إِبْراهِيمُ رَبِّ اجْعَلْ هَذَا الْبَلَدَ آمِناً — When Ibrahim said, ‘My Lord! Make this city secure’ (14:35). The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "a father" (والِدٍ) is for veneration and magnification (التعظيم والتفخيم). The expression "and that which he begot" (وَما وَلَدَ) instead of "and him whom he begot" (ومن ولد) is to indicate wonder at his affair by way of praise, as in His saying: وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِما وَضَعَتْ — and Allah knew best what she had given birth to (3:36).

The meaning is: I swear by a father of great stature, who is Ibrahim, and by a child whom he begot of wondrous affair and blessed impact, who is Isma’il his son; they are the two builders of this town. Thus, the import of the three verses is the swearing by Noble Mecca, by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) who is a resident therein, and by Ibrahim and Isma’il who built it.

It has been said that the "father" refers to Ibrahim and "that which he begot" refers to all his descendants among the Arabs. However, it is far-fetched that Allah the Glorified would pair the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and Ibrahim (peace be upon him) with the likes of Abu Lahab, Abu Jahl, and other leaders of disbelief (أئمة الكفر), swearing by them all in one context. Ibrahim (peace be upon him) himself disassociated from those of his children who did not follow him in monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — when he said, as Allah recounted: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّكُ مِنِّي وَمَنْ عَصانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — and keep me and my children away from worshipping idols. My Lord! They have indeed misled many of the people. So whoever follows me indeed belongs to me, and as for him who disobeys me, well, You are indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful (14:35-36).

Therefore, whoever interprets "that which he begot" as the children of Ibrahim must restrict them to the Muslims among his progeny, as in the supplication of Ibrahim and Isma’il when they were building the Kaaba, as Allah recounted: رَبَّنا وَاجْعَلْنا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّتِنا أُمَّةً مُسْلِمَةً لَكَ وَأَرِنا مَناسِكَنا وَتُبْ عَلَيْنا — Our Lord, make us submissive to You, and [raise] from our progeny a nation submissive to You, and show us our holy rites of worship, and turn to us clemently (2:128).

It has also been said that "the father and him whom he begot" refers to Adam (peace be upon him) and all his progeny, on the grounds that the object of these oaths is the creation of man in toil. Allah has established the law of birth (سنة الولادة) in the creation of this species and the preservation of its existence. Thus, He swore in these verses by the product of this law—the father and that which he begot—that man is in labor and toil according to the nature of his creation from the moment he lives until the moment he dies. This view is not bad in itself, but there remains the need to explain the suitability between the town of Mecca and "a father and every offspring" in combining them in the oaths.

It has also been said that they refer to Adam and the righteous among his progeny; the reason for this would be to transcend Allah the Exalted from swearing by His tyrannical enemies and the corrupt among the disbelievers and the profligate. It has also been said that they refer to every father and every offspring. It has also been said: he who begets and he who does not beget among them, by taking the "ma" (ما) in "ma walada" (ما وَلَدَ) as negative rather than relative. It has also been said that the "father" is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and "that which he begot" is his community (أمة), because he is in the position of a father to his community. These are far-fetched views.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ فِي كَبَدٍ — certainly We created man in travail (90:4). Kabad (الكبد) is labor and toil. The sentence is the response to the oath (جواب القسم). The fact that toil encompasses the creation of man and that labor and exhaustion surround him in all affairs of his life is not hidden from any person of intellect. He does not intend any blessing of the blessings of the world except that it be pure in its goodness and unalloyed in its pleasantness, yet he attains nothing of them except that it is mixed with what disturbs life, coupled with endurance and struggle, in addition to the calamities of time that afflict him and the sudden occurrences of events that surprise him.

His saying, the Exalted: أَيَحْسَبُ أَنْ لَنْ يَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ أَحَدٌ — Does he suppose that no one will ever have power over him? (90:5). This is in the position of a conclusion to the argument of the previous verse. Its explanation is that since man's creation is built upon toil—which is his container—and he never attains anything he wants except less than what he wants or other than what he wants, he is encompassed in his creation, defeated in his will, and overpowered in what has been decreed for him. The One who defeats him in his will and overpowers him to engage in what He has decreed for him is Allah the Glorified; He has power over him from every side, so He may dispose of him as He wills and take him when He intends.

Thus, it is not for man to suppose that no one will ever have power over him, such that this leads him to exalt himself over Allah and wax proud against His worship, or to give in some of what He has commanded—such as spending in His way—and then consider it much and boast of it before Allah, or to plot against Allah the Exalted after performing an act for the sake of the Noble Countenance out of ostentation and seeking reputation, saying: "I have consumed vast wealth."

His saying, the Exalted: يَقُولُ أَهْلَكْتُ مالاً لُبَداً — He says, ‘I have squandered a wealth in abundance’ (90:6). Lubad (اللبد) means much. The context of the verse and the verses following it until the end of the Surah suggests that there was someone who had manifested Islam or inclined toward it, and had spent some of his wealth and then boasted of it, considering it a great amount, by saying: "I have consumed vast wealth." Thus the verses were revealed, and Allah refuted him by stating that attaining the "right-handedness" (ميمنة) of life is not achieved except by surmounting the uphill task (عقبة) of spending in the way of Allah and entering the fold of those who believe and enjoin one another to patience and mercy. This is supported by what will follow in the traditional inquiry, Allah the Exalted willing.

His saying, the Exalted: أَيَحْسَبُ أَنْ لَمْ يَرَهُ أَحَدٌ — Does he suppose that no one has seen him? (90:7). This is a denial of what is necessitated by the man's saying, "I have consumed vast wealth," by way of metonymy (التكنية). The gist of the meaning is that the implication of the man's informing others of his squandering vast wealth is that he supposes We are in heedlessness and ignorance of what he has spent. He has erred in that, for Allah the Glorified is All-Seeing of what he spent. However, this amount is not sufficient for attaining the "right-handedness" of life; rather, he must bear more than that of the hardships of servitude (العبودية), so he must surmount the uphill task and be with the believers in all that they are in.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُ عَيْنَيْنِ وَلِساناً وَشَفَتَيْنِ وَهَدَيْناهُ النَّجْدَيْنِ — Have We not made for him two eyes, a tongue, and two lips, and shown him the two paths? (90:8-10). The path (النجد) is the elevated road. What is meant by "the two paths" (النجدين) are the path of good and the path of evil. They are called najdayn because of the effort and labor involved in traversing either of them. They have also been interpreted as the mother's two breasts, but this is far-fetched.

His saying: أَلَمْ نَجْعَلْ لَهُ عَيْنَيْنِ means: Have We not equipped him in his body with that by which he sees, so that knowledge of visible things is attained for him across their vast scope? His saying: وَلِساناً وَشَفَتَيْنِ means: Or have We not made for him a tongue and two lips with which he seeks assistance in speaking and indicating the knowledge within his conscience, whereby others are guided to the knowledge of matters hidden from sight?

His saying: وَهَدَيْناهُ النَّجْدَيْنِ means: We taught him the path of good and the path of evil through inspiration (إلهام) from Us; thus he knows good and distinguishes it from evil. The verse is similar in meaning to His saying: وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها — and the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its wickedness and its godwariness (91:7-8).

In these three verses is an argument against his saying: أَيَحْسَبُ أَنْ لَمْ يَرَهُ أَحَدٌ; that is, an argument that He the Exalted sees the deeds of His servants, knows the types of actions within their consciences, and distinguishes good from evil and the good deed from the bad.

The gist of it is that Allah the Glorified is the One who makes visible things known to man by means of his two eyes; how can it be conceived that He makes something known to him while He Himself does not know it? He is the One who points man to what is in the conscience by means of speech; is it rational that He reveals to him what is veiled from Himself? He is the One who teaches man and distinguishes for him good and evil through inspiration; is it possible along with this that He Himself does not know it nor distinguish it? Thus, He the Exalted sees what man has done, knows what he intends by his deed, and distinguishes whether it is good or evil, a good deed or a bad one.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَا اقْتَحَمَ الْعَقَبَةَ — Yet he has not embarked upon the uphill task (90:11). Iqtiham (الاقتحام) is to enter with speed, pressure, and intensity. The 'aqabah (العقبة) is the difficult, rugged path that involves ascending a mountain. Embarking upon the uphill task is an allusion to spending wealth, which is difficult for the spender, as will be explicitly stated.

It has been said that the sentence is a supplication against the man who said, "I have consumed vast wealth," but this is of no consequence.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الْعَقَبَةُ — And what will show you what the uphill task is? (90:12). This is to magnify its status, as has been mentioned in similar instances.

His saying, the Exalted: فَكُّ رَقَبَةٍ — It is the freeing of a slave (90:13). That is, its manumission and liberation. Or the estimation is: "It—meaning the uphill task—is the freeing of a slave." Thus, what is meant by the uphill task is the act of freeing itself, and embarking upon it is performing it, and performing the act is the act itself.

From this, the error of some who said that "freeing a slave" is the embarking upon the uphill task and not the uphill task itself becomes apparent; they claim there is a deleted possessed noun (مضاف) to which the pronoun refers, the estimation being: "And what will show you what the embarking upon the uphill task is? It—meaning the embarking—is the freeing of a slave."

What was mentioned in the explanation of the uphill task—the freeing of a slave and the feeding on a day of starvation—are among the instances of spreading mercy (نشر الرحمة), specifically mentioned due to their importance. Freeing a slave was mentioned first and begun with due to the complete concern of the religion for the liberation of slaves.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ إِطْعامٌ فِي يَوْمٍ ذِي مَسْغَبَةٍ يَتِيماً ذا مَقْرَبَةٍ أَوْ مِسْكِيناً ذا مَتْرَبَةٍ — or feeding on a day of starvation an orphan among relatives, or a needy man in desolation (90:14-16). Masghabah (المسغبة) is famine. Maqrabah (المقربة) is kinship by lineage. Matrabah (المتربة) is from turab (dust), meaning clinging to the dust due to the intensity of poverty. The meaning is: or feeding on a day of famine an orphan from among the relatives or a severely poor person.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ كانَ مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَتَواصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ وَتَواصَوْا بِالْمَرْحَمَةِ — while being one of those who have faith and enjoin one another to patience, and enjoin one another to compassion (90:17). Marhamah (المرحمة) is a verbal noun (مصدر ميمي) from rahmah (mercy). Enjoining one another to patience (التواصي بالصبر) is the recommendation of some to others to be patient in obedience to Allah. Enjoining one another to compassion (التواصي بالمرحمة) is the recommendation of some to others to show mercy to those of poverty, need, and destitution.

The sentence, meaning His saying: ثُمَّ كانَ etc., is coordinated (معطوفة) with the saying: اقْتَحَمَ. The estimation is: "He did not embark upon the uphill task, nor was he among those who believe..." etc. Other views have been mentioned regarding this which are of no benefit.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ — They are the People of the Right Hand (90:18). This is in the sense of yumn (blessedness) as opposed to shu'm (ill fortune). The demonstration "those" (أولئك) refers to what the previous context indicates; that is, those who embarked upon the uphill task and were among those who believed and enjoined one another to patience and mercy are the people of blessedness; they see nothing from what they have sent forth of faith and righteous deeds except a blessed, beautiful, and pleasing matter.

It has been said that what is meant by maymanah (الميمنة) is the right side, and the People of the Right Hand are those who are given their records in their right hands. However, the contrast between maymanah and mash'amah (the left/ill fortune) does not suit this.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِنا هُمْ أَصْحابُ الْمَشْأَمَةِ — But those who defy Our signs, they are the People of the Left Hand (90:19). The signs (الآيات) in the horizons and within the souls are signs and proofs of Him the Exalted, indicating His uniqueness in Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية) and all that branches from them. Rejecting them is disbelief (كفر) in them, and disbelief in them is disbelief in Allah. Likewise is the Noble Quran and its verses, and likewise what was revealed and conveyed through the path of the Message.

It appears that what is meant by the "signs" is all of them in general. Mash'amah (المشأمة) is the opposite of maymanah.

His saying, the Exalted: عَلَيْهِمْ نارٌ مُؤْصَدَةٌ — with a closed fire [enveloping] them (90:20). That is, closed over them.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma’, regarding His saying: وَأَنْتَ حِلٌّ بِهذَا الْبَلَدِ, it was said: Its meaning is that you are muhill (محل) in this town, which is the opposite of muhrim (محرم). What is meant is: it is permissible for you to kill whom you see of the disbelievers. That was when he was commanded to fight on the day of the conquest of Mecca—so Allah made it permissible for him until he fought and killed. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "It was not made permissible for anyone before me, nor will it be made permissible for anyone after me—and it was not made permissible for me except for an hour of a day." Narrated from Ibn Abbas, Mujahid, and ‘Ata.

In the same source, regarding the verse, it was said: "I swear by this town—while you are halal (حلال), your sanctity violated, your honor made permissible, and you are not respected—so no sanctity remains for the town when you are violated." This is from Abu Muslim, and it is what is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

He said: "The Quraysh used to venerate the town but would treat Muhammad as permissible therein. So He said: لا أُقْسِمُ بِهذَا الْبَلَدِ وَأَنْتَ حِلٌّ بِهذَا الْبَلَدِ, meaning they treated you as permissible therein, they belied you, and they reviled you. Yet a man among them would not seize the killer of his father therein—and they would wear the bark of the trees of the Sanctuary—so they would be secure by wearing it. Thus, they treated as permissible regarding the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) what they did not treat as permissible regarding anyone else—so Allah censured them for that."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَوالِدٍ وَما وَلَدَ, it was said: "Adam and what he begot of prophets, successors (الأوصياء), and their followers." Narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

I say: The previous meanings are narrated through Sunni chains in mawquf traditions. Al-Qummi narrated the last two in his Tafsir as mursal and mudmar narrations.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: يَقُولُ أَهْلَكْتُ مالاً لُبَداً — He said: Lubad means accumulated. In al-Majma’, regarding the verse, it was said: "It refers to al-Harith ibn Nawfal ibn ‘Abd Manaf—that is because he committed a sin and sought a ruling from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—so he commanded him to perform an expiation. He said: 'My wealth has gone in expiations and expenditures since I entered the religion of Muhammad.'" Narrated from Muqatil.

In al-Majma’: It was said to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him): "Some people say regarding His saying: وَهَدَيْناهُ النَّجْدَيْنِ that they are the two breasts." He said: "No, they are good and evil."

In Usul al-Kafi, with its chain to Hamzah ibn Muhammad from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), he said: I asked him about the saying of Allah the Exalted: وَهَدَيْناهُ النَّجْدَيْنِ. He said: "The path (نجد) of good and evil."

I say: This meaning is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur through various chains from ‘Ali (peace be upon him), Anas, Abu Umamah, and others from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Al-Qummi also narrated it in his Tafsir as a mursal and mudmar narration.

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Ja’far ibn Khallad, he said: Whenever Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) ate, a large bowl would be brought and placed near his table. He would go to the best of the food brought to him, take a bit of everything, and place it in that bowl. Then he would order it to be given to the poor. Then he would recite this verse: فَلَا اقْتَحَمَ الْعَقَبَةَ.

Then he would say: "Allah, Mighty and Majestic, knew that not every person is capable of freeing a slave, so He made for them a way to Paradise."

In al-Majma’, it is narrated as a marfu’ tradition from al-Bara’ ibn ‘Azib, who said: A Bedouin came to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, teach me a deed that will enter me into Paradise." He said: "Though you have made the speech short, you have broadened the question. Liberate the soul (النسمة) and free the slave (الرقبة)." He asked: "Are they not one and the same?" He replied: "No. Liberating the soul is to manumit it alone, and freeing the slave is to assist in its price. And [give] the surplus (الفيء) to the relative who is unjust. If that is not possible, then feed the hungry and give drink to the thirsty, and enjoin the good and forbid the evil. If you cannot bear that, then restrain your tongue except from good."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَوْ مِسْكِيناً ذا مَتْرَبَةٍ, he said: "Nothing protects him from the dust."
